The Substackerati - Columbia Journalism Review

Columbia Journalism Review · by Clio Chang

The first week of March, Patrice Peck, a freelance journalist living in New York, started sanitizing everything. She went to Nitehawk, a dine-in movie theater, and brought Clorox to wipe down the little table by her seat, her drinking glass, the utensils. In those early days, she felt like she was the only one obsessing over the coronavirus. As the pandemic spread, she started exchanging updates with a friend via text message and calling her grandmother in Jamaica to discuss the situation there. Peck anticipated that Black people would be hit the hardest, and that this aspect of the story would not receive enough coverage. \xe2\x80\x9cIt was just very obvious to me,\xe2\x80\x9d she said.

By April, shelter-in-place orders were in effect. Peck\xe2\x80\x94who is thirty-three and stylish, lately with cat-eye glasses and short hair\xe2\x80\x94was holed up in her apartment. She and her partner set up to work side by side, their laptops perched on the kitchen island; Peck scoured the internet for news as Black people in America began dying from covid-19, the disease caused by the coronavirus, at twice the rate of whites. \xe2\x80\x9cI wanted to write something that would be valuable to readers and informative and empowering, particularly to Black audiences,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. So she did what so many other independent journalists were doing\xe2\x80\x94she started a Substack.

Substack, established in 2017 by three tech-and-media guys\xe2\x80\x94Chris Best, Hamish McKenzie, and Jairaj Sethi\xe2\x80\x94is a newsletter platform that allows writers and other creative types to distribute their work at tiered subscription rates. Newsletters go back at least as far as the Middle Ages, but these days, with full-time jobs at stable media companies evaporating\xe2\x80\x94between the 2008 recession and 2019, newsroom employment dropped by 23 percent\xe2\x80\x94Substack offers an appealing alternative. And, for many, it\xe2\x80\x99s a viable source of income. In three years, Substack\xe2\x80\x99s newsletters\xe2\x80\x94covering almost every conceivable topic, from Australian Aboriginal rights to bread recipes to local Tennessee politics\xe2\x80\x94have drawn more than two hundred fifty thousand paid subscribers. The top newsletter authors can earn six figures, an unheard-of amount for freelance journalists. Emily Atkin, who runs Heated, on the climate crisis, told me that her gross annual income surpassed $200,000\xe2\x80\x94and among paid-readership Substacks, she\xe2\x80\x99s ranked fifteenth. \xe2\x80\x9cI literally opened my first savings account,\xe2\x80\x9d she said.

Peck had been mulling the idea of starting a newsletter for a while. She began thinking seriously about Substack when she saw Beauty IRL, a newsletter by Darian Harvin. Like Peck, Harvin is a freelancer\xe2\x80\x94it was \xe2\x80\x9creally a matter of time\xe2\x80\x9d until she was laid off from one media job or another, she figured\xe2\x80\x94and she was using Substack as a place for surplus ideas. \xe2\x80\x9cI take some of my pitches and just write them for my newsletter,\xe2\x80\x9d Harvin said. \xe2\x80\x9cPublications are only paying me three hundred dollars per piece, so I thought, What would happen if I took some of them and grew my audience?\xe2\x80\x9d Her efforts were getting noticed; eventually, Substack gave her a $3,000 stipend and a $25,000 advance (in the latter arrangement, Substack takes 50 percent of her subscription fees until the advance is paid off, but if she doesn\xe2\x80\x99t reach that number, Harvin won\xe2\x80\x99t owe Substack the rest).

Peck settled on a name for her project: Coronavirus News for Black Folks. She\xe2\x80\x99d produce it a few times a week, with a reading list of recommended articles and original interviews with Black essential workers, accompanied by images she\xe2\x80\x99d commission from Black illustrators. At least at the start, she decided, her newsletter would be free\xe2\x80\x94she wanted her writing to be accessible, especially as Black people were suffering disproportionately from the pandemic-induced economic downturn. Her first installments were about Black men afraid of being racially profiled for wearing masks and coronavirus conspiracies circulating online; the tone was direct and conversational. (\xe2\x80\x9cAs Belcalis Marlenis Alm\xc3\xa1nzar famously said about covid-19, \xe2\x80\x98Sh*t is getting real,\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x89\xe2\x80\x9d Peck wrote in an early dispatch.) After a couple weeks, Adriana Lacy, another Black journalist, interviewed Peck for her own Substack\xe2\x80\x94The Intersection, focused on journalism, technology, and innovation\xe2\x80\x94and Nieman Lab picked it up. A month into developing her newsletter, Peck had nine hundred seventy-eight subscribers. Not long after, that number rose to two thousand.

Peck quickly recognized the possibilities of Substack: a wandering journalist, disenchanted by an industry that was never all that equitable to begin with and is now in financial free fall, could, perhaps, claim control of her work. As more people signed up for Coronavirus News for Black Folks, Peck imagined all the ways it might grow, and wondered whether it could become a full-time job. \xe2\x80\x9cIn an ideal world, I\xe2\x80\x99m the editor in chief or editor at large of the newsletter; I\xe2\x80\x99m using it to allow other journalists who like to cover these communities to have a place to write,\xe2\x80\x9d she mused. \xe2\x80\x9cAnd I\xe2\x80\x99m able to compensate them during a time when there are so many layoffs.\xe2\x80\x9d

Patrice Peck When she started Coronavirus News for Black Folks, Peck was the sole producer of an ambitious project.

Substack started the same way many media ventures do\xe2\x80\x94with a personal essay. In 2017, Best, a programmer from a Vancouver suburb who cofounded a messaging app called Kik, had taken some time off work and, finding himself an avid reader, began thinking about writing something himself. He drafted a piece bemoaning how the journalism industry\xe2\x80\x99s failing business models incentivized clicks, retweets, and likes over incisive prose. At the time, the media apocalypse was in full force\xe2\x80\x94the limits of digital media were apparent (that year, Mic and Vice instituted mass layoffs after bowing to Facebook in an ill-fated \xe2\x80\x9cpivot to video\xe2\x80\x9d) and legacy media was bleeding (Cond\xc3\xa9 Nast faced perennial ad revenue loss; a desperate Tribune Publishing changed its name to Tronc). \xe2\x80\x9cNow we\xe2\x80\x99re in this world where social media feeds optimize for engagement, because that\xe2\x80\x99s how they make money, and just as kind of an unintentional collateral damage they end up amplifying all the things that drive us crazy,\xe2\x80\x9d Best argued. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s bad for us as readers and bad for society.\xe2\x80\x9d Best sent a first draft to McKenzie, with whom he\xe2\x80\x99d worked at Kik.

\xe2\x80\x9cHe was like, \xe2\x80\x98First of all, you\xe2\x80\x99re a bad writer and you shouldn\xe2\x80\x99t do this,\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x89\xe2\x80\x9d Best recalled. In McKenzie\xe2\x80\x99s telling, he gently informed Best that he thought he was stating the obvious: everyone in media already understood what the problems were; what was missing was a solution. Before working in technology, McKenzie had grown up in a small town on the South Island of New Zealand and attended journalism school with the hope of becoming a foreign correspondent. He spent four years in Hong Kong, where he ended up writing mostly about indie music and drinking dens. Later, he worked as a reporter for PandoDaily, a tech news site, then transitioned into writing for companies\xe2\x80\x94first Tesla, then Kik. McKenzie encouraged Best to think about more than a diagnosis. Through the spring of 2017, the two sent emails back and forth, had video calls, and brainstormed in Google Docs about what models might better serve journalism. Subscriptions, they decided, seemed the most promising\xe2\x80\x94but not in the form of journals or magazines. \xe2\x80\x9cPaid newsletters\xe2\x80\x9d felt more familiar, personal, trustworthy\xe2\x80\x94and more monetizable.

They had good reason to think it could work. Best and McKenzie were both fans of Stratechery, the newsletter by Ben Thompson, a former employee of Apple and Microsoft based in Taipei, who since 2014 had been writing about tech full-time, charging readers directly. \xe2\x80\x9cThis guy was writing this newsletter from his bedroom in Taiwan and, as far as we know, making like a million dollars a year,\xe2\x80\x9d Best said. (An exaggeration, perhaps, but Thompson was earning a solid living.) They wondered why his approach, which took advantage of the internet\xe2\x80\x99s strengths\xe2\x80\x94a global distribution network, easy payment systems\xe2\x80\x94hadn\xe2\x80\x99t been replicated more widely. The Skimm and Axios had built companies around monetizing newsletters, but it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t an idea widely embraced by individual journalists.

The guys devised a system of taking a 10 percent cut from subscriptions (Stripe, the credit card service that processed the fees, would take 2.9 percent, plus thirty cents per transaction), which they felt tied them to the writers. They reached out to Sethi, a developer they knew from Kik, to build out the technology. Their tagline was \xe2\x80\x9cWe literally only make money when the writers do.\xe2\x80\x9d Substack\xe2\x80\x99s mission, announced upon the company\xe2\x80\x99s official debut, sounded more grandiose: \xe2\x80\x9cWhen it has reached maturity, the subscription-based news industry could well be much larger than the newspaper business ever was, much like the ride-hailing industry in San Francisco is bigger than the taxi industry was before Lyft and Uber,\xe2\x80\x9d the founders wrote. \xe2\x80\x9cDemocratizing this subscription-based future will enable more writers to earn more money by writing about what truly matters. It puts the media\xe2\x80\x99s destiny into the right hands.\xe2\x80\x9d

To get the future started, they recruited some contributors. The first was Bill Bishop, someone McKenzie knew from his time in Hong Kong. Bishop already ran a popular free newsletter, Sinocism, analyzing China-related news, and was thinking about going behind a paywall. He agreed to move his subscriber base\xe2\x80\x94thirty thousand readers\xe2\x80\x94to Substack. On launch day, in October 2017, he turned his newsletter into a six-figure business. (Bishop also became an angel investor in Substack.)

In the winter of 2018, the founders successfully applied for seed funding from Y Combinator, a company that helps startups get off the ground. By the summer of 2019, they announced that they had raised $15.3 million in Series A funding, with Andreessen Horowitz, the venture capital firm, as the lead backer. \xe2\x80\x9cSubstack can solve the structural issues between publishers/writers and readers in a way that aligns the incentives between all of them,\xe2\x80\x9d Andrew Chen, a general partner at Andreessen Horowitz, wrote at the time. \xe2\x80\x9cThis is the moment the next generation of media is being built.\xe2\x80\x9d Chen joined Substack\xe2\x80\x99s board; they signed a lease on an office in San Francisco. As more people signed up to join the Substackerati, the company garnered praise from journalists. \xe2\x80\x9cSubstack represents a radically different alternative, in which the \xe2\x80\x98media company\xe2\x80\x99 is a service and the journalists are in charge,\xe2\x80\x9d Ben Smith wrote in the New York Times. A Taste Media piece, anointing newsletters as the future of food coverage, argued that Substack is \xe2\x80\x9callowing voices to be heard\xe2\x80\x94through simple and free publishing tools\xe2\x80\x94but it also allows creators to flip the switch for monetization.\xe2\x80\x9d Last year, BuzzFeed\xe2\x80\x99s Alex Kantrowitz wrote that \xe2\x80\x9cpaid email newsletters can bring in real money for writers with small, dedicated subscriber bases\xe2\x80\x9d; this year, Kantrowitz announced that he was leaving BuzzFeed to start a Substack.

Including McKenzie, who is thirty-nine; Best, thirty-three; and Sethi, thirty-one, the team now comprises seventeen people. Since the pandemic started, they\xe2\x80\x99ve let the lease on their headquarters lapse. Recently, McKenzie and Best met with me over Zoom from their respective makeshift home setups. Both wore soft-gray T-shirts, the kind that represent the day-to-night look of media company founders everywhere; Best was working on a beard. Their lives had changed a lot since the spring\xe2\x80\x94not just because of the coronavirus, they explained; each of them had newborns. (\xe2\x80\x9cChris and I have dueling babies; they arrived within ten days of each other,\xe2\x80\x9d McKenzie said.) It was also the case that the pandemic had boosted their company\xe2\x80\x99s growth\xe2\x80\x94in the first three months, as hundreds of journalists lost their jobs, the number of active writers on Substack doubled and revenue increased by 60 percent. In the same week in July, Substack was covered in the New York Times and the Washington Post. McKenzie told me, \xe2\x80\x9cThere\xe2\x80\x99s been a huge \xe2\x80\x98Oh, everyone\xe2\x80\x99s paying attention to Substack now\xe2\x80\x99 kind of feeling.\xe2\x80\x9d

They continued to approach potential contributors. When I asked what, exactly, they thought made someone a promising Substack writer, Best turned to McKenzie and asked, in a jokey hush, \xe2\x80\x9cDo we keep the Baschez score a secret?\xe2\x80\x9d McKenzie laughed. They have a system, created by a former employee named Nathan Baschez, that measures a Twitter user\xe2\x80\x99s engagement level\xe2\x80\x94retweets, likes, replies\xe2\x80\x94among their followers. This person is then assigned a score on a logarithmic scale of fire emojis. Four fire emojis is very good\xe2\x80\x94Substack material. Best and McKenzie will reach out and suggest that the person try a newsletter. The four-fire-emoji method turned up Heather Cox Richardson, a history professor at Boston College, whose Substack, Letters from an American\xe2\x80\x94political with a historical eye\xe2\x80\x94is now the second-top-paid. (The most popular newsletter on Substack is The Dispatch, a conservative publication founded by Jonah Goldberg, Steve Hayes, and Toby Stock.) \xe2\x80\x9cWe called it giving them \xe2\x80\x98the religion,\xe2\x80\x99 because it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t about Let\xe2\x80\x99s type into this box and make money because people will pay you,\xe2\x80\x9d McKenzie said, of recruits. \xe2\x80\x9cIt was like, We think there\xe2\x80\x99s going to be a cultural shift here.\xe2\x80\x9d Lately, it seemed like everyone was a convert.

Because newsletter creators retain control of their email list, archives, and intellectual property, Substack\xe2\x80\x99s main selling point is independence\xe2\x80\x94from bosses, from ad-dependent corporate media models, from the whims of tech monopolies like Google and Facebook. The founders don\xe2\x80\x99t claim that Substack will \xe2\x80\x9csave\xe2\x80\x9d media\xe2\x80\x94a promise that\xe2\x80\x99s bound to disappoint\xe2\x80\x94but they argue that their model is a core part of a better, more worker-centric and reader-friendly future for journalism. All of that was attractive to Peck, who had decided in 2019 to leave a staff job at BuzzFeed, where she had been a beauty writer covering race, identity, diversity, and intersectional representation for underrepresented audiences. She\xe2\x80\x99d had a bad experience there; after a round of layoffs, she was shuffled into a content-farm role; her new manager was unsupportive. She felt like the media industry offered her few alternatives (\xe2\x80\x9cWhere can we go, as Black journalists?\xe2\x80\x9d she wondered aloud), so she quit her job to figure out what might come next. Ultimately, she decided to work only for herself.

That, of course, has its downsides. A hallmark of freelance life is isolation. Peck soon found that the labor of producing a newsletter can be grueling. Because Coronavirus News for Black Folks includes outside links, it requires lots of time-intensive reading\xe2\x80\x94more than three days\xe2\x80\x99 worth, if she doesn\xe2\x80\x99t cram. The editing, compiling, and writing requires discipline; sometimes she stays up all night to finish. Then there\xe2\x80\x99s production and the rest. Peck gradually slowed her pace, sending out installments a few times per month. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m creating graphics on Instagram to promote it, tweeting it, doing everything,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s a one-woman show. That gets exhausting. I don\xe2\x80\x99t put it out as frequently as I\xe2\x80\x99d like to.\xe2\x80\x9d

Complicating work was life during a pandemic: in the middle of the spring, Peck began living part-time in Los Angeles, where her partner had gotten a job. Without vacation days, she kept on filing new dispatches\xe2\x80\x94about the pandemic\xe2\x80\x99s effect in the Caribbean, about a pharmacist living paycheck to paycheck and receiving a \xe2\x80\x9cpitiful excuse\xe2\x80\x9d for hazard pay. She kept accumulating subscribers. But she realized how the intensity of her efforts, and the fact that the newsletter fell entirely on her shoulders, could lead quickly to burnout.

Writing is often considered an individualistic enterprise, but journalism is a collective endeavor. And that is the paradox of Substack: it\xe2\x80\x99s a way out of a newsroom\xe2\x80\x94and the racism or harassment or vulture-venture capitalism one encountered there\xe2\x80\x94but it\xe2\x80\x99s all the way out, on one\xe2\x80\x99s own. \xe2\x80\x9cHoly shit, I work anywhere from fifty to sixty hours a week,\xe2\x80\x9d Atkin, of Heated, told me. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s a lot.\xe2\x80\x9d Harvin, the Beauty IRL writer, said she missed the infrastructure\xe2\x80\x94legal and editorial\xe2\x80\x94of a traditional outlet. \xe2\x80\x9cI just know how valuable it is to have a second ear to bounce ideas off of, someone to challenge you,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m very not big into writing in a vacuum, and I think that is the thing I miss the most.\xe2\x80\x9d Kelsey McKinney, a journalist whose literary Substack, Written Out, has accounted for about a third of her income during the pandemic, doesn\xe2\x80\x99t do any reporting for her newsletter because of the lack of legal and editorial backing. Investigative journalism seems particularly difficult as a solo enterprise on Substack, which doesn\xe2\x80\x99t reward slowly developed, uncertain projects that come out sporadically.

Substack has taken a few steps to address these concerns. Using its venture funding, the company has offered financial assistance to some newsletter writers\xe2\x80\x94from small, no-strings-attached cash grants to $25,000 advances and $100,000 fellowships. In July, it introduced the Substack Defender program, through which writers with paid subscriptions could apply for third-party legal support. In the announcement, the founders promised more to come: \xe2\x80\x9cWe will make a large investment in a services program that includes initiatives related to healthcare, personal finance, editing, distribution, design, and coworking spaces.\xe2\x80\x9d What all that entails, exactly, McKenzie told me they\xe2\x80\x99re still figuring out; for now, they\xe2\x80\x99ve started a pilot program to connect writers with editors and healthcare. \xe2\x80\x9cThe solution to helping support a healthy ecosystem for writers and journalists is not a beautiful CMS or the blockchain or any other gimmicky thing,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s the entire support structure.\xe2\x80\x9d A few newsletters, finding the corporate help insufficient, created their own iterations of newsrooms that use Substack mainly as a platform to publish (The Dispatch, for instance).

\xe2\x80\x9cSubstack is not the sort of thing that is going to create a sustainable next phase, but it can open the door to things that we don\xe2\x80\x99t have doors for yet,\xe2\x80\x9d Nathan Schneider, a media studies professor at the University of Colorado, Boulder, told me. To the extent that Substack fixes something in the journalism industry, it might be compared to GoFundMe\xe2\x80\x94a survival mechanism whose resources are unevenly, arbitrarily distributed, laying bare systemic problems without directly tackling them. \xe2\x80\x9cGoFundMe can help us see things we\xe2\x80\x99re not seeing and put money where it would not go,\xe2\x80\x9d Schneider said. \xe2\x80\x9cOf course, we don\xe2\x80\x99t want a GoFundMe society.\xe2\x80\x9d

In my conversations with Substack writers, most told me that their newsletters didn\xe2\x80\x99t amount to full-time work; they still had to hold on to other gigs. Peck earns her income primarily from one-off freelance pieces and speaking opportunities; she\xe2\x80\x99s only been able to take a break from regular employment thanks to the support of her partner. Some writers use their Substacks to promote other projects they\xe2\x80\x99ve been working on. A few consider it a place to get weird (see: Ellie Shechet\xe2\x80\x99s Horrible Lists, with entries like \xe2\x80\x9cHow to give up on your dream of moving home to become an herb farmer in 11 easy steps\xe2\x80\x9d). J.P. Brammer, who moved his popular advice column \xc2\xa1Hola Papi! from one magazine to another before arriving at Substack, called it his \xe2\x80\x9cretirement home from queer media.\xe2\x80\x9d When he\xe2\x80\x99s not writing his newsletter, he\xe2\x80\x99s finishing up a memoir. He appreciates Substack for what it is. \xe2\x80\x9cIf there wasn\xe2\x80\x99t a flood, you wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t need to build a dam,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cYou wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t be like, \xe2\x80\x98Oh, I love this dam.\xe2\x80\x99 It\xe2\x80\x99s sort of something you have to do to get by.\xe2\x80\x9d

Illustration by Olivia Fields. Click to enlarge.

If you visit Substack\xe2\x80\x99s website, you\xe2\x80\x99ll see leaderboards of the top twenty-five paid and free newsletters; the writers\xe2\x80\x99 names are accompanied by their little circular avatars. The intention is declarative\xe2\x80\x94you, too, can make it on Substack. But as you peruse the lists, something becomes clear: the most successful people on Substack are those who have already been well-served by existing media power structures. Most are white and male; several are conservative. Matt Taibbi, Andrew Sullivan, and most recently, Glenn Greenwald\xe2\x80\x94who offer similar screeds about the dangers of cancel culture and the left\xe2\x80\x94all land in the top ten. (Greenwald\xe2\x80\x99s arrival bumped the like-minded Yascha Mounk to eleventh position; soon, Matthew Yglesias signed up for Substack, too.)

None of that is so surprising\xe2\x80\x94it\xe2\x80\x99s hard to earn four-fire-emoji status without having already built up a reputation within established institutions. And, as this year\xe2\x80\x99s anti-racist activism has made all the more visible, those institutions are built from prejudiced systems, which form working environments that are often unsustainable for people who are nonwhite or non-elite. \xe2\x80\x9cI think one of the reasons why we often see that the top-twenty-five board at Substack is mostly white authors is because that\xe2\x80\x99s an extension of the type of audience and recognition they get for their work on other platforms,\xe2\x80\x9d Harvin said.

Peck was not among those recruited to join Substack. It was only when she started to get publicity that McKenzie tweeted about her project. She\xe2\x80\x99s never appeared on Substack\xe2\x80\x99s homepage, nor has she heard from any of the founders directly. \xe2\x80\x9cI think Substack should make it easier to discover newsletters on their platform,\xe2\x80\x9d she told me. The way the top-twenty-five lists are organized, she believes, is \xe2\x80\x9cto the detriment of Black journalists.\xe2\x80\x9d (McKenzie told me that Substack will soon revamp its leaderboards, highlighting top revenue earners in different categories.)

In general, will Substack replicate the patterns of marginalization found across the media industry, or will it help people locked out of the dominant media sphere to flourish? To a large extent, the answer depends on whether or not Substack\xe2\x80\x99s founders believe they\xe2\x80\x99re in the publishing business. When we spoke, they were adamant that Substack is a platform, not a media company\xe2\x80\x94a familiar refrain of Silicon Valley media ventures. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99re not hiring writers, and we\xe2\x80\x99re not publishing editorial,\xe2\x80\x9d McKenzie said. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99re enabling writers and enabling editorial.\xe2\x80\x9d He told me that the leaderboards, which were originally conceived to show writers what kind of \xe2\x80\x9cquality work\xe2\x80\x9d was being done on Substack, were organized by audience and revenue metrics, with \xe2\x80\x9cno thumb on the scale\xe2\x80\x9d from the company. When I asked about their views on content moderation, the founders said that, because readers opt in to newsletters\xe2\x80\x94unlike Facebook, there\xe2\x80\x99s no algorithm-based feed\xe2\x80\x94they have relatively less responsibility to get involved.

It\xe2\x80\x99s a bit of a brain twister: Substack, eager to attract customers over Mailchimp or WordPress, has begun to look like it\xe2\x80\x99s reverse engineering a media company. But all the while, its founders insist that they simply provide a platform. By not acknowledging the ways in which they are actively encouraging (and discouraging) certain people to use Substack, and the ways they benefit monetarily from doing so, they obscure their role as publishers. As Study Hall\xe2\x80\x99s Allegra Hobbs put it over the summer, \xe2\x80\x9cIt seems the creators of Substack, in their zeal to become the future of media, are trying to have it both ways\xe2\x80\x94to keep an appropriate editorial distance while also actively supporting writers beyond merely providing a space to publish.\xe2\x80\x9d

In addition, like many media companies, Substack is dependent on large amounts of venture capital. Time and again, journalists have seen venture capitalists barge in on their newsrooms with claims that they\xe2\x80\x99ll solve the industry\xe2\x80\x99s problems, only to end up losing their jobs or being forced to churn out clickbait. (In the case of Substack, The Atlantic\xe2\x80\x99s Kaitlyn Tiffany has argued that tech bros are monetizing an existing form of media\xe2\x80\x94newsletters\xe2\x80\x94that had long been used, especially by women, to foster communities that were \xe2\x80\x9cnon-remunerative\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9cartistically strange.\xe2\x80\x9d) Substack\xe2\x80\x99s founders are open about the fact that media and VC money typically don\xe2\x80\x99t mix well; McKenzie told me that journalists who are VC-skeptical feel \xe2\x80\x9cburned for good reasons.\xe2\x80\x9d But he said there was a difference between companies like BuzzFeed and Vox Media taking hundreds of millions in venture capital \xe2\x80\x9con a big unproven bet that that can scale to a massive return\xe2\x80\x9d and Substack, which he calls \xe2\x80\x9ca platform that has a stable, transparent, and simple business model that is proven to work.\xe2\x80\x9d When I asked if Substack\xe2\x80\x99s investors were looking for large returns, Best replied, \xe2\x80\x9cWe have expectations for growth for ourselves that are at least as high as our investors\xe2\x80\x99.\xe2\x80\x9d

Even if you accept that premise, there remains a broader question\xe2\x80\x94one that the industry at large will have to answer\xe2\x80\x94as to whether venture capitalism, driven by the pursuit of high returns on big-bet investments, is, at its core, antithetical to the project of journalism. (The reasons Peck made her newsletter free run at odds with the goals of investors.) I asked Substack\xe2\x80\x99s founders about the sentiment, popular among the venture capitalist class, that reporters are too powerful and need to be curbed. \xe2\x80\x9cOur business is a little bound up with Andreessen Horowitz, and our business is a lot bound up with writers,\xe2\x80\x9d McKenzie said. \xe2\x80\x9cWe don\xe2\x80\x99t look to control or influence the thought of either of those groups. We don\xe2\x80\x99t own the attitudes of every Substack writer, and we don\xe2\x80\x99t own the attitudes of our investors.\xe2\x80\x9d

It was a nonideological, noneditorial stance\xe2\x80\x94one that he\xe2\x80\x99d taken in conversation with me before. But often, adherence to neutrality only enforces existing power structures. In these moments, Substack\xe2\x80\x99s founders veer into unsettling corporate-tech-dude-speak, papering over the fact that a \xe2\x80\x9cnonideological\xe2\x80\x9d vision is, of course, ideology just the same. When Sullivan joined Substack, over the summer, he put the company\xe2\x80\x99s positioning to the test: infamous for publishing excerpts from The Bell Curve, a book that promotes bigoted race \xe2\x80\x9cscience,\xe2\x80\x9d Sullivan would now produce the Weekly Dish, a political newsletter. (Substack\xe2\x80\x99s content guidelines draw a line at hate speech.) Sullivan\xe2\x80\x99s Substack quickly rose to become the fifth-most-read among paid subscriptions\xe2\x80\x94he claimed that his income had risen from less than $200,000 at New York magazine to $500,000. When I asked the founders if they thought his presence might discourage other writers from joining, they gave me a pat reply. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99re not a media company,\xe2\x80\x9d Best said. \xe2\x80\x9cIf somebody joins the company and expects us to have an editorial position and be rigorously enforcing some ideological line, this is probably not the company they wanted to join in the first place.\xe2\x80\x9d

In a broken industry, even a little agency can start to feel like control. But that won\xe2\x80\x99t necessarily translate into the large-scale transformation that Substack\xe2\x80\x99s founders pitch. If \xe2\x80\x9cbe your own boss\xe2\x80\x9d is a nice slogan in the abstract, it ignores the fact that power dynamics always exist, even where they\xe2\x80\x99re not formalized. As time went on, Peck came up against the limits of what she could do alone. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s great I don\xe2\x80\x99t have to go to anybody for approving stories or things I want to cover,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cBut I think it\xe2\x80\x99s always beneficial having other professional journalists to work with and bounce ideas off of and get feedback from.\xe2\x80\x9d In August, to help with global coverage, she brought on a contributing editor; for any original reporting he does, she pays out of pocket. She wants to keep the newsletter free, though to keep herself going, she\xe2\x80\x99s considering adding an option for subscribers to pay.

Ideally, however, her future wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t involve Substack at all, Peck told me; if Coronavirus News for Black Folks were picked up by a major outlet, say, she\xe2\x80\x99d have the staff and resources to build it out properly. That\xe2\x80\x99s an unlikely scenario, she knows\xe2\x80\x94for the same reasons that she was driven out of the mainstream media industry to begin with. \xe2\x80\x9cI got into journalism because I wanted to write stories about and for the Black community,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cThere\xe2\x80\x99s not a lot of places where you can do that and get paid a decent amount and have benefits. It varies, but I don\xe2\x80\x99t think a lot of Black journalists have a ton of options outside of creating their own things for themselves.\xe2\x80\x9d What she has now is a harbor. \xe2\x80\x9cSubstack has some of the materials for free to help us to build our own thing,\xe2\x80\x9d she continued. \xe2\x80\x9cWe need so much more, but we\xe2\x80\x99re going to work with what we have.\xe2\x80\x9d

As more journalists embark on independent careers, the need for support infrastructure, beyond Substack, will become increasingly urgent. Labor organizing, the traditional method for making an industry more equitable, will have to adapt to the new conditions, especially as more and more industries embrace the independent-contractor model. Accountability is harder when the company you work for refuses to acknowledge what field it\xe2\x80\x99s operating in. Yet people like Peck are still workers, even if they lack a boss.

In September, Discourse Blog, a newsletter on the politics and culture of the left run by a group of journalists who used to work at a now-discontinued site called Splinter (I used to write for it, too), decided they would leave Substack for a competitor called Lede. It was the third time in six months that Discourse, which launched on WordPress, had changed platforms\xe2\x80\x94perhaps an indication of some of the difficulties of creating ambitious projects independently, even as a team. When they made the announcement, the writers at Discourse said that, at Substack, they were limited in their ability to grow. One of the co-owners noted that it was hard to attract readers through internet search alone; they wanted to track audience data. In essence, they sought, as much as possible, to steer their own destiny.

The guys at Substack aren\xe2\x80\x99t sweating the loss, at least for now. Ultimately, they will be judged not by their creative output, but by how much money they can return for those who have invested in their company. The platform is new, but the metrics are not; financial concerns trump all others. When I asked Best and McKenzie about their plans post-pandemic (should that time ever come), they told me that they don\xe2\x80\x99t foresee any changes to their fundamentals. \xe2\x80\x9cWe did not build Substack to be successful only during disaster times,\xe2\x80\x9d McKenzie said. In a recent column, Ben Smith, of the Times, reported that Twitter has discussed acquiring Substack, though McKenzie quickly tweeted, \xe2\x80\x9cThis is not going to happen.\xe2\x80\x9d

They\xe2\x80\x99re still scouting writers. \xe2\x80\x9cDo you have a Substack?\xe2\x80\x9d Best asked me, at one point. \xe2\x80\x9cAlways be closing, Chris,\xe2\x80\x9d McKenzie said, with a grin. I smiled and gave a noncommittal answer. As a freelancer, it seemed more likely than not that one day I would start a Substack, or something similar. What choice did I have?

Clio Chang is a freelance reporter based in New York. She writes about politics, culture, media, and more.
Columbia Journalism Review · by Clio Chang
 
T he first week of March, Patrice Peck, a freelance journalist living in New York, started sanitizing everything. She went to Nitehawk, a dine- in movie theater, and brought Clorox to wipe down the little table by her seat, her drinking glass, the utensils. In those early days, she felt like she was the only one obsessing over the coronavirus. As the pandemic spread, she started exchanging updates with a friend via text message and calling her grandmother in Jamaica to discuss the situation there. Peck anticipated that Black people would be hit the hardest, and that this aspect of the story would not receive enough coverage. \xe2\x80\x9cIt was just very obvious to me,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. By April, shelter- in- place orders were in effect. Peck\xe2\x80\x94 who is thirty- three and stylish, lately with cat- eye glasses and short hair\xe2\x80\x94 was holed up in her apartment. She and her partner set up to work side by side, their laptops perched on the kitchen island; Peck scoured the internet for news as Black people in America began dying from covid- 19 , the disease caused by the coronavirus, at twice the rate of whites. \xe2\x80\x9cI wanted to write something that would be valuable to readers and informative and empowering, particularly to Black audiences,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. So she did what so many other independent journalists were doing\xe2\x80\x94 she started a Substack. Substack, established in 2017 by three tech- and- media guys\xe2\x80\x94 Chris Best, Hamish McKenzie, and Jairaj Sethi\xe2\x80\x94 is a newsletter platform that allows writers and other creative types to distribute their work at tiered subscription rates. Newsletters go back at least as far as the Middle Ages, but these days, with full- time jobs at stable media companies evaporating\xe2\x80\x94 between the 2008 recession and 2019, newsroom employment dropped by 23 percent\xe2\x80\x94 Substack offers an appealing alternative. And, for many, it\xe2\x80\x99s a viable source of income. In three years, Substack\xe2\x80\x99s newsletters\xe2\x80\x94 covering almost every conceivable topic, from Australian Aboriginal rights to bread recipes to local Tennessee politics\xe2\x80\x94 have drawn more than two hundred fifty thousand paid subscribers. The top newsletter authors can earn six figures, an unheard- of amount for freelance journalists. Emily Atkin, who runs Heated , on the climate crisis, told me that her gross annual income surpassed $200,000\xe2\x80\x94 and among paid- readership Substacks, she\xe2\x80\x99s ranked fifteenth. \xe2\x80\x9cI literally opened my first savings account,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. Peck had been mulling the idea of starting a newsletter for a while. She began thinking seriously about Substack when she saw Beauty IRL , a newsletter by Darian Harvin. Like Peck, Harvin is a freelancer\xe2\x80\x94 it was \xe2\x80\x9creally a matter of time\xe2\x80\x9d until she was laid off from one media job or another, she figured\xe2\x80\x94 and she was using Substack as a place for surplus ideas. \xe2\x80\x9cI take some of my pitches and just write them for my newsletter,\xe2\x80\x9d Harvin said. \xe2\x80\x9cPublications are only paying me three hundred dollars per piece, so I thought, What would happen if I took some of them and grew my audience? \xe2\x80\x9d Her efforts were getting noticed; eventually, Substack gave her a $3,000 stipend and a $25,000 advance (in the latter arrangement, Substack takes 50 percent of her subscription fees until the advance is paid off, but if she doesn\xe2\x80\x99t reach that number, Harvin won\xe2\x80\x99t owe Substack the rest). Peck settled on a name for her project: Coronavirus News for Black Folks . She\xe2\x80\x99d produce it a few times a week, with a reading list of recommended articles and original interviews with Black essential workers, accompanied by images she\xe2\x80\x99d commission from Black illustrators. At least at the start, she decided, her newsletter would be free\xe2\x80\x94 she wanted her writing to be accessible, especially as Black people were suffering disproportionately from the pandemic- induced economic downturn. Her first installments were about Black men afraid of being racially profiled for wearing masks and coronavirus conspiracies circulating online; the tone was direct and conversational. (\xe2\x80\x9cAs Belcalis Marlenis Alm\xc3\xa1nzar famously said about covid- 19 , \xe2\x80\x98Sh*t is getting real,\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Peck wrote in an early dispatch.) After a couple weeks, Adriana Lacy, another Black journalist, interviewed Peck for her own Substack\xe2\x80\x94 The Intersection , focused on journalism, technology, and innovation\xe2\x80\x94 and Nieman Lab picked it up. A month into developing her newsletter, Peck had nine hundred seventy- eight subscribers. Not long after, that number rose to two thousand. Peck quickly recognized the possibilities of Substack: a wandering journalist, disenchanted by an industry that was never all that equitable to begin with and is now in financial free fall, could, perhaps, claim control of her work. As more people signed up for Coronavirus News for Black Folks , Peck imagined all the ways it might grow, and wondered whether it could become a full- time job. \xe2\x80\x9cIn an ideal world, I\xe2\x80\x99m the editor in chief or editor at large of the newsletter; I\xe2\x80\x99m using it to allow other journalists who like to cover these communities to have a place to write,\xe2\x80\x9d she mused. \xe2\x80\x9cAnd I\xe2\x80\x99m able to compensate them during a time when there are so many layoffs.\xe2\x80\x9d Patrice Peck When she started Coronavirus News for Black Folks, Peck was the sole producer of an ambitious project. S ubstack started the same way many media ventures do\xe2\x80\x94 with a personal essay. In 2017, Best, a programmer from a Vancouver suburb who cofounded a messaging app called Kik, had taken some time off work and, finding himself an avid reader, began thinking about writing something himself. He drafted a piece bemoaning how the journalism industry\xe2\x80\x99s failing business models incentivized clicks, retweets, and likes over incisive prose. At the time, the media apocalypse was in full force\xe2\x80\x94 the limits of digital media were apparent (that year, Mic and Vice instituted mass layoffs after bowing to Facebook in an ill- fated \xe2\x80\x9cpivot to video\xe2\x80\x9d) and legacy media was bleeding (Cond\xc3\xa9 Nast faced perennial ad revenue loss; a desperate Tribune Publishing changed its name to Tronc). \xe2\x80\x9cNow we\xe2\x80\x99re in this world where social media feeds optimize for engagement, because that\xe2\x80\x99s how they make money, and just as kind of an unintentional collateral damage they end up amplifying all the things that drive us crazy,\xe2\x80\x9d Best argued. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s bad for us as readers and bad for society.\xe2\x80\x9d Best sent a first draft to McKenzie, with whom he\xe2\x80\x99d worked at Kik. \xe2\x80\x9cHe was like, \xe2\x80\x98First of all, you\xe2\x80\x99re a bad writer and you shouldn\xe2\x80\x99t do this,\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Best recalled. In McKenzie\xe2\x80\x99s telling, he gently informed Best that he thought he was stating the obvious: everyone in media already understood what the problems were; what was missing was a solution. Before working in technology, McKenzie had grown up in a small town on the South Island of New Zealand and attended journalism school with the hope of becoming a foreign correspondent. He spent four years in Hong Kong, where he ended up writing mostly about indie music and drinking dens. Later, he worked as a reporter for PandoDaily , a tech news site, then transitioned into writing for companies\xe2\x80\x94 first Tesla, then Kik. McKenzie encouraged Best to think about more than a diagnosis. Through the spring of 2017, the two sent emails back and forth, had video calls, and brainstormed in Google Docs about what models might better serve journalism. Subscriptions, they decided, seemed the most promising\xe2\x80\x94 but not in the form of journals or magazines. \xe2\x80\x9cPaid newsletters\xe2\x80\x9d felt more familiar, personal, trustworthy\xe2\x80\x94 and more monetizable. They had good reason to think it could work. Best and McKenzie were both fans of Stratechery , the newsletter by Ben Thompson, a former employee of Apple and Microsoft based in Taipei, who since 2014 had been writing about tech full- time, charging readers directly. \xe2\x80\x9cThis guy was writing this newsletter from his bedroom in Taiwan and, as far as we know, making like a million dollars a year,\xe2\x80\x9d Best said. (An exaggeration, perhaps, but Thompson was earning a solid living.) They wondered why his approach, which took advantage of the internet\xe2\x80\x99s strengths\xe2\x80\x94 a global distribution network, easy payment systems\xe2\x80\x94 hadn\xe2\x80\x99t been replicated more widely. The Skimm and Axios had built companies around monetizing newsletters, but it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t an idea widely embraced by individual journalists. The guys devised a system of taking a 10 percent cut from subscriptions (Stripe, the credit card service that processed the fees, would take 2.9 percent, plus thirty cents per transaction), which they felt tied them to the writers. They reached out to Sethi, a developer they knew from Kik, to build out the technology. Their tagline was \xe2\x80\x9cWe literally only make money when the writers do.\xe2\x80\x9d Substack\xe2\x80\x99s mission, announced upon the company\xe2\x80\x99s official debut, sounded more grandiose: \xe2\x80\x9cWhen it has reached maturity, the subscription- based news industry could well be much larger than the newspaper business ever was, much like the ride- hailing industry in San Francisco is bigger than the taxi industry was before Lyft and Uber,\xe2\x80\x9d the founders wrote. \xe2\x80\x9cDemocratizing this subscription- based future will enable more writers to earn more money by writing about what truly matters. It puts the media\xe2\x80\x99s destiny into the right hands.\xe2\x80\x9d To get the future started, they recruited some contributors. The first was Bill Bishop, someone McKenzie knew from his time in Hong Kong. Bishop already ran a popular free newsletter, Sinocism , analyzing China- related news, and was thinking about going behind a paywall. He agreed to move his subscriber base\xe2\x80\x94 thirty thousand readers\xe2\x80\x94 to Substack. On launch day, in October 2017, he turned his newsletter into a six- figure business. (Bishop also became an angel investor in Substack.) In the winter of 2018, the founders successfully applied for seed funding from Y Combinator, a company that helps startups get off the ground. By the summer of 2019, they announced that they had raised $15.3 million in Series A funding, with Andreessen Horowitz, the venture capital firm, as the lead backer. \xe2\x80\x9cSubstack can solve the structural issues between publishers writers and readers in a way that aligns the incentives between all of them,\xe2\x80\x9d Andrew Chen, a general partner at Andreessen Horowitz, wrote at the time. \xe2\x80\x9cThis is the moment the next generation of media is being built.\xe2\x80\x9d Chen joined Substack\xe2\x80\x99s board; they signed a lease on an office in San Francisco. As more people signed up to join the Substackerati, the company garnered praise from journalists. \xe2\x80\x9cSubstack represents a radically different alternative, in which the \xe2\x80\x98media company\xe2\x80\x99 is a service and the journalists are in charge,\xe2\x80\x9d Ben Smith wrote in the New York Times . A Taste Media piece, anointing newsletters as the future of food coverage, argued that Substack is \xe2\x80\x9callowing voices to be heard\xe2\x80\x94 through simple and free publishing tools\xe2\x80\x94 but it also allows creators to flip the switch for monetization.\xe2\x80\x9d Last year, BuzzFeed \xe2\x80\x99s Alex Kantrowitz wrote that \xe2\x80\x9cpaid email newsletters can bring in real money for writers with small, dedicated subscriber bases\xe2\x80\x9d; this year, Kantrowitz announced that he was leaving BuzzFeed to start a Substack. Including McKenzie, who is thirty- nine; Best, thirty- three; and Sethi, thirty- one, the team now comprises seventeen people. Since the pandemic started, they\xe2\x80\x99ve let the lease on their headquarters lapse. Recently, McKenzie and Best met with me over Zoom from their respective makeshift home setups. Both wore soft- gray T- shirts, the kind that represent the day- to- night look of media company founders everywhere; Best was working on a beard. Their lives had changed a lot since the spring\xe2\x80\x94 not just because of the coronavirus, they explained; each of them had newborns. (\xe2\x80\x9cChris and I have dueling babies; they arrived within ten days of each other,\xe2\x80\x9d McKenzie said.) It was also the case that the pandemic had boosted their company\xe2\x80\x99s growth\xe2\x80\x94 in the first three months, as hundreds of journalists lost their jobs, the number of active writers on Substack doubled and revenue increased by 60 percent. In the same week in July, Substack was covered in the New York Times and the Washington Post . McKenzie told me, \xe2\x80\x9cThere\xe2\x80\x99s been a huge \xe2\x80\x98Oh, everyone\xe2\x80\x99s paying attention to Substack now\xe2\x80\x99 kind of feeling.\xe2\x80\x9d They continued to approach potential contributors. When I asked what, exactly, they thought made someone a promising Substack writer, Best turned to McKenzie and asked, in a jokey hush, \xe2\x80\x9cDo we keep the Baschez score a secret?\xe2\x80\x9d McKenzie laughed. They have a system, created by a former employee named Nathan Baschez, that measures a Twitter user\xe2\x80\x99s engagement level\xe2\x80\x94 retweets, likes, replies\xe2\x80\x94 among their followers. This person is then assigned a score on a logarithmic scale of fire emojis. Four fire emojis is very good\xe2\x80\x94 Substack material. Best and McKenzie will reach out and suggest that the person try a newsletter. The four- fire- emoji method turned up Heather Cox Richardson, a history professor at Boston College, whose Substack, Letters from an American political with a historical eye\xe2\x80\x94 is now the second- top- paid. (The most popular newsletter on Substack is The Dispatch , a conservative publication founded by Jonah Goldberg, Steve Hayes, and Toby Stock.) \xe2\x80\x9cWe called it giving them \xe2\x80\x98the religion,\xe2\x80\x99 because it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t about Let\xe2\x80\x99s type into this box and make money because people will pay you ,\xe2\x80\x9d McKenzie said, of recruits. \xe2\x80\x9cIt was like, We think there\xe2\x80\x99s going to be a cultural shift here .\xe2\x80\x9d Lately, it seemed like everyone was a convert. B ecause newsletter creators retain control of their email list, archives, and intellectual property, Substack\xe2\x80\x99s main selling point is independence\xe2\x80\x94 from bosses, from ad- dependent corporate media models, from the whims of tech monopolies like Google and Facebook. The founders don\xe2\x80\x99t claim that Substack will \xe2\x80\x9csave\xe2\x80\x9d media\xe2\x80\x94 a promise that\xe2\x80\x99s bound to disappoint\xe2\x80\x94 but they argue that their model is a core part of a better, more worker- centric and reader- friendly future for journalism. All of that was attractive to Peck, who had decided in 2019 to leave a staff job at BuzzFeed , where she had been a beauty writer covering race, identity, diversity, and intersectional representation for underrepresented audiences. She\xe2\x80\x99d had a bad experience there; after a round of layoffs, she was shuffled into a content- farm role; her new manager was unsupportive. She felt like the media industry offered her few alternatives (\xe2\x80\x9cWhere can we go, as Black journalists?\xe2\x80\x9d she wondered aloud), so she quit her job to figure out what might come next. Ultimately, she decided to work only for herself. That, of course, has its downsides. A hallmark of freelance life is isolation. Peck soon found that the labor of producing a newsletter can be grueling. Because Coronavirus News for Black Folks includes outside links, it requires lots of time- intensive reading\xe2\x80\x94 more than three days\xe2\x80\x99 worth, if she doesn\xe2\x80\x99t cram. The editing, compiling, and writing requires discipline; sometimes she stays up all night to finish. Then there\xe2\x80\x99s production and the rest. Peck gradually slowed her pace, sending out installments a few times per month. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m creating graphics on Instagram to promote it, tweeting it, doing everything,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s a one- woman show. That gets exhausting. I don\xe2\x80\x99t put it out as frequently as I\xe2\x80\x99d like to.\xe2\x80\x9d Complicating work was life during a pandemic: in the middle of the spring, Peck began living part- time in Los Angeles, where her partner had gotten a job. Without vacation days, she kept on filing new dispatches\xe2\x80\x94 about the pandemic\xe2\x80\x99s effect in the Caribbean, about a pharmacist living paycheck to paycheck and receiving a \xe2\x80\x9cpitiful excuse\xe2\x80\x9d for hazard pay. She kept accumulating subscribers. But she realized how the intensity of her efforts, and the fact that the newsletter fell entirely on her shoulders, could lead quickly to burnout. Writing is often considered an individualistic enterprise, but journalism is a collective endeavor. And that is the paradox of Substack: it\xe2\x80\x99s a way out of a newsroom\xe2\x80\x94 and the racism or harassment or vulture- venture capitalism one encountered there\xe2\x80\x94 but it\xe2\x80\x99s all the way out, on one\xe2\x80\x99s own. \xe2\x80\x9cHoly shit, I work anywhere from fifty to sixty hours a week,\xe2\x80\x9d Atkin, of Heated , told me. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s a lot.\xe2\x80\x9d Harvin, the Beauty IRL writer, said she missed the infrastructure\xe2\x80\x94 legal and editorial\xe2\x80\x94 of a traditional outlet. \xe2\x80\x9cI just know how valuable it is to have a second ear to bounce ideas off of, someone to challenge you,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m very not big into writing in a vacuum, and I think that is the thing I miss the most.\xe2\x80\x9d Kelsey McKinney, a journalist whose literary Substack, Written Out , has accounted for about a third of her income during the pandemic, doesn\xe2\x80\x99t do any reporting for her newsletter because of the lack of legal and editorial backing. Investigative journalism seems particularly difficult as a solo enterprise on Substack, which doesn\xe2\x80\x99t reward slowly developed, uncertain projects that come out sporadically. Substack has taken a few steps to address these concerns. Using its venture funding, the company has offered financial assistance to some newsletter writers\xe2\x80\x94 from small, no- strings- attached cash grants to $25,000 advances and $100,000 fellowships. In July, it introduced the Substack Defender program, through which writers with paid subscriptions could apply for third- party legal support. In the announcement, the founders promised more to come: \xe2\x80\x9cWe will make a large investment in a services program that includes initiatives related to healthcare, personal finance, editing, distribution, design, and coworking spaces.\xe2\x80\x9d What all that entails, exactly, McKenzie told me they\xe2\x80\x99re still figuring out; for now, they\xe2\x80\x99ve started a pilot program to connect writers with editors and healthcare. \xe2\x80\x9cThe solution to helping support a healthy ecosystem for writers and journalists is not a beautiful CMS or the blockchain or any other gimmicky thing,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s the entire support structure.\xe2\x80\x9d A few newsletters, finding the corporate help insufficient, created their own iterations of newsrooms that use Substack mainly as a platform to publish ( The Dispatch , for instance). \xe2\x80\x9cSubstack is not the sort of thing that is going to create a sustainable next phase, but it can open the door to things that we don\xe2\x80\x99t have doors for yet,\xe2\x80\x9d Nathan Schneider, a media studies professor at the University of Colorado, Boulder, told me. To the extent that Substack fixes something in the journalism industry, it might be compared to GoFundMe\xe2\x80\x94 a survival mechanism whose resources are unevenly, arbitrarily distributed, laying bare systemic problems without directly tackling them. \xe2\x80\x9cGoFundMe can help us see things we\xe2\x80\x99re not seeing and put money where it would not go,\xe2\x80\x9d Schneider said. \xe2\x80\x9cOf course, we don\xe2\x80\x99t want a GoFundMe society.\xe2\x80\x9d In my conversations with Substack writers, most told me that their newsletters didn\xe2\x80\x99t amount to full- time work; they still had to hold on to other gigs. Peck earns her income primarily from one- off freelance pieces and speaking opportunities; she\xe2\x80\x99s only been able to take a break from regular employment thanks to the support of her partner. Some writers use their Substacks to promote other projects they\xe2\x80\x99ve been working on. A few consider it a place to get weird (see: Ellie Shechet\xe2\x80\x99s Horrible Lists , with entries like \xe2\x80\x9cHow to give up on your dream of moving home to become an herb farmer in 11 easy steps\xe2\x80\x9d). J.P. Brammer, who moved his popular advice column \xc2\xa1Hola Papi! from one magazine to another before arriving at Substack, called it his \xe2\x80\x9cretirement home from queer media.\xe2\x80\x9d When he\xe2\x80\x99s not writing his newsletter, he\xe2\x80\x99s finishing up a memoir. He appreciates Substack for what it is. \xe2\x80\x9cIf there wasn\xe2\x80\x99t a flood, you wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t need to build a dam,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cYou wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t be like, \xe2\x80\x98Oh, I love this dam.\xe2\x80\x99 It\xe2\x80\x99s sort of something you have to do to get by.\xe2\x80\x9d Illustration by Olivia Fields. Click to enlarge. I f you visit Substack\xe2\x80\x99s website, you\xe2\x80\x99ll see leaderboards of the top twenty- five paid and free newsletters; the writers\xe2\x80\x99 names are accompanied by their little circular avatars. The intention is declarative\xe2\x80\x94 you, too, can make it on Substack . But as you peruse the lists, something becomes clear: the most successful people on Substack are those who have already been well- served by existing media power structures. Most are white and male; several are conservative. Matt Taibbi, Andrew Sullivan, and most recently, Glenn Greenwald\xe2\x80\x94 who offer similar screeds about the dangers of cancel culture and the left\xe2\x80\x94 all land in the top ten. (Greenwald\xe2\x80\x99s arrival bumped the like- minded Yascha Mounk to eleventh position; soon, Matthew Yglesias signed up for Substack, too.) None of that is so surprising\xe2\x80\x94 it\xe2\x80\x99s hard to earn four- fire- emoji status without having already built up a reputation within established institutions. And, as this year\xe2\x80\x99s anti- racist activism has made all the more visible, those institutions are built from prejudiced systems, which form working environments that are often unsustainable for people who are nonwhite or non- elite. \xe2\x80\x9cI think one of the reasons why we often see that the top- twenty- five board at Substack is mostly white authors is because that\xe2\x80\x99s an extension of the type of audience and recognition they get for their work on other platforms,\xe2\x80\x9d Harvin said. Peck was not among those recruited to join Substack. It was only when she started to get publicity that McKenzie tweeted about her project. She\xe2\x80\x99s never appeared on Substack\xe2\x80\x99s homepage, nor has she heard from any of the founders directly. \xe2\x80\x9cI think Substack should make it easier to discover newsletters on their platform,\xe2\x80\x9d she told me. The way the top- twenty- five lists are organized, she believes, is \xe2\x80\x9cto the detriment of Black journalists.\xe2\x80\x9d (McKenzie told me that Substack will soon revamp its leaderboards, highlighting top revenue earners in different categories.) In general, will Substack replicate the patterns of marginalization found across the media industry, or will it help people locked out of the dominant media sphere to flourish? To a large extent, the answer depends on whether or not Substack\xe2\x80\x99s founders believe they\xe2\x80\x99re in the publishing business. When we spoke, they were adamant that Substack is a platform, not a media company\xe2\x80\x94 a familiar refrain of Silicon Valley media ventures. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99re not hiring writers, and we\xe2\x80\x99re not publishing editorial,\xe2\x80\x9d McKenzie said. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99re enabling writers and enabling editorial.\xe2\x80\x9d He told me that the leaderboards, which were originally conceived to show writers what kind of \xe2\x80\x9cquality work\xe2\x80\x9d was being done on Substack, were organized by audience and revenue metrics, with \xe2\x80\x9cno thumb on the scale\xe2\x80\x9d from the company. When I asked about their views on content moderation, the founders said that, because readers opt in to newsletters\xe2\x80\x94 unlike Facebook, there\xe2\x80\x99s no algorithm- based feed\xe2\x80\x94 they have relatively less responsibility to get involved. It\xe2\x80\x99s a bit of a brain twister: Substack, eager to attract customers over Mailchimp or WordPress, has begun to look like it\xe2\x80\x99s reverse engineering a media company. But all the while, its founders insist that they simply provide a platform. By not acknowledging the ways in which they are actively encouraging (and discouraging) certain people to use Substack, and the ways they benefit monetarily from doing so, they obscure their role as publishers. As Study Hall \xe2\x80\x99s Allegra Hobbs put it over the summer, \xe2\x80\x9cIt seems the creators of Substack, in their zeal to become the future of media, are trying to have it both ways\xe2\x80\x94 to keep an appropriate editorial distance while also actively supporting writers beyond merely providing a space to publish.\xe2\x80\x9d In addition, like many media companies, Substack is dependent on large amounts of venture capital. Time and again, journalists have seen venture capitalists barge in on their newsrooms with claims that they\xe2\x80\x99ll solve the industry\xe2\x80\x99s problems, only to end up losing their jobs or being forced to churn out clickbait. (In the case of Substack, The Atlantic \xe2\x80\x99s Kaitlyn Tiffany has argued that tech bros are monetizing an existing form of media\xe2\x80\x94 newsletters\xe2\x80\x94 that had long been used, especially by women, to foster communities that were \xe2\x80\x9cnon- remunerative\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9cartistically strange.\xe2\x80\x9d) Substack\xe2\x80\x99s founders are open about the fact that media and VC money typically don\xe2\x80\x99t mix well; McKenzie told me that journalists who are VC- skeptical feel \xe2\x80\x9cburned for good reasons.\xe2\x80\x9d But he said there was a difference between companies like BuzzFeed and Vox Media taking hundreds of millions in venture capital \xe2\x80\x9con a big unproven bet that that can scale to a massive return\xe2\x80\x9d and Substack, which he calls \xe2\x80\x9ca platform that has a stable, transparent, and simple business model that is proven to work.\xe2\x80\x9d When I asked if Substack\xe2\x80\x99s investors were looking for large returns, Best replied, \xe2\x80\x9cWe have expectations for growth for ourselves that are at least as high as our investors\xe2\x80\x99.\xe2\x80\x9d Even if you accept that premise, there remains a broader question\xe2\x80\x94 one that the industry at large will have to answer\xe2\x80\x94 as to whether venture capitalism, driven by the pursuit of high returns on big- bet investments, is, at its core, antithetical to the project of journalism. (The reasons Peck made her newsletter free run at odds with the goals of investors.) I asked Substack\xe2\x80\x99s founders about the sentiment, popular among the venture capitalist class, that reporters are too powerful and need to be curbed. \xe2\x80\x9cOur business is a little bound up with Andreessen Horowitz, and our business is a lot bound up with writers,\xe2\x80\x9d McKenzie said. \xe2\x80\x9cWe don\xe2\x80\x99t look to control or influence the thought of either of those groups. We don\xe2\x80\x99t own the attitudes of every Substack writer, and we don\xe2\x80\x99t own the attitudes of our investors.\xe2\x80\x9d It was a nonideological, noneditorial stance\xe2\x80\x94 one that he\xe2\x80\x99d taken in conversation with me before. But often, adherence to neutrality only enforces existing power structures. In these moments, Substack\xe2\x80\x99s founders veer into unsettling corporate- tech- dude- speak, papering over the fact that a \xe2\x80\x9cnonideological\xe2\x80\x9d vision is, of course, ideology just the same. When Sullivan joined Substack, over the summer, he put the company\xe2\x80\x99s positioning to the test: infamous for publishing excerpts from The Bell Curve , a book that promotes bigoted race \xe2\x80\x9cscience,\xe2\x80\x9d Sullivan would now produce the Weekly Dish , a political newsletter. (Substack\xe2\x80\x99s content guidelines draw a line at hate speech.) Sullivan\xe2\x80\x99s Substack quickly rose to become the fifth- most- read among paid subscriptions\xe2\x80\x94 he claimed that his income had risen from less than $200,000 at New York magazine to $500,000. When I asked the founders if they thought his presence might discourage other writers from joining, they gave me a pat reply. \xe2\x80\x9cWe\xe2\x80\x99re not a media company,\xe2\x80\x9d Best said. \xe2\x80\x9cIf somebody joins the company and expects us to have an editorial position and be rigorously enforcing some ideological line, this is probably not the company they wanted to join in the first place.\xe2\x80\x9d I n a broken industry, even a little agency can start to feel like control. But that won\xe2\x80\x99t necessarily translate into the large- scale transformation that Substack\xe2\x80\x99s founders pitch. If \xe2\x80\x9cbe your own boss\xe2\x80\x9d is a nice slogan in the abstract, it ignores the fact that power dynamics always exist, even where they\xe2\x80\x99re not formalized. As time went on, Peck came up against the limits of what she could do alone. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s great I don\xe2\x80\x99t have to go to anybody for approving stories or things I want to cover,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cBut I think it\xe2\x80\x99s always beneficial having other professional journalists to work with and bounce ideas off of and get feedback from.\xe2\x80\x9d In August, to help with global coverage, she brought on a contributing editor; for any original reporting he does, she pays out of pocket. She wants to keep the newsletter free, though to keep herself going, she\xe2\x80\x99s considering adding an option for subscribers to pay. Ideally, however, her future wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t involve Substack at all, Peck told me; if Coronavirus News for Black Folks were picked up by a major outlet, say, she\xe2\x80\x99d have the staff and resources to build it out properly. That\xe2\x80\x99s an unlikely scenario, she knows\xe2\x80\x94 for the same reasons that she was driven out of the mainstream media industry to begin with. \xe2\x80\x9cI got into journalism because I wanted to write stories about and for the Black community,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cThere\xe2\x80\x99s not a lot of places where you can do that and get paid a decent amount and have benefits. It varies, but I don\xe2\x80\x99t think a lot of Black journalists have a ton of options outside of creating their own things for themselves.\xe2\x80\x9d What she has now is a harbor. \xe2\x80\x9cSubstack has some of the materials for free to help us to build our own thing,\xe2\x80\x9d she continued. \xe2\x80\x9cWe need so much more, but we\xe2\x80\x99re going to work with what we have.\xe2\x80\x9d As more journalists embark on independent careers, the need for support infrastructure, beyond Substack, will become increasingly urgent. Labor organizing, the traditional method for making an industry more equitable, will have to adapt to the new conditions, especially as more and more industries embrace the independent- contractor model. Accountability is harder when the company you work for refuses to acknowledge what field it\xe2\x80\x99s operating in. Yet people like Peck are still workers, even if they lack a boss. In September, Discourse Blog , a newsletter on the politics and culture of the left run by a group of journalists who used to work at a now- discontinued site called Splinter (I used to write for it, too), decided they would leave Substack for a competitor called Lede. It was the third time in six months that Discourse , which launched on WordPress, had changed platforms\xe2\x80\x94 perhaps an indication of some of the difficulties of creating ambitious projects independently, even as a team. When they made the announcement, the writers at Discourse said that, at Substack, they were limited in their ability to grow. One of the co- owners noted that it was hard to attract readers through internet search alone; they wanted to track audience data. In essence, they sought, as much as possible, to steer their own destiny. The guys at Substack aren\xe2\x80\x99t sweating the loss, at least for now. Ultimately, they will be judged not by their creative output, but by how much money they can return for those who have invested in their company. The platform is new, but the metrics are not; financial concerns trump all others. When I asked Best and McKenzie about their plans post- pandemic (should that time ever come), they told me that they don\xe2\x80\x99t foresee any changes to their fundamentals. \xe2\x80\x9cWe did not build Substack to be successful only during disaster times,\xe2\x80\x9d McKenzie said. In a recent column, Ben Smith, of the Times , reported that Twitter has discussed acquiring Substack, though McKenzie quickly tweeted, \xe2\x80\x9cThis is not going to happen.\xe2\x80\x9d They\xe2\x80\x99re still scouting writers. \xe2\x80\x9cDo you have a Substack?\xe2\x80\x9d Best asked me, at one point. \xe2\x80\x9cAlways be closing, Chris,\xe2\x80\x9d McKenzie said, with a grin. I smiled and gave a noncommittal answer. As a freelancer, it seemed more likely than not that one day I would start a Substack, or something similar. What choice did I have? Clio Chang is a freelance reporter based in New York. She writes about politics, culture, media, and more.  \xe2\x97\x86
T he first week of March, Patrice Peck, a freelance journalist living in New York, started sanitizing everything. She went to Nitehawk, a dine- in movie theater, and brought Clorox to wipe down the little table by her seat, her drinking glass, the utensils. In those early days, she felt like she was the only one obsessing over the coronavirus. As the pandemic spread, she started exchanging updates with a friend via text message and calling her grandmother in Jamaica to discuss the situation there. Peck anticipated that Black people would be hit the hardest, and that this aspect of the story would not receive enough coverage. \xe2\x80\x9cIt was just very obvious to me,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. By April, shelter- in- place orders were in effect. Peck\xe2\x80\x94 who is thirty- three and stylish, lately with cat- eye glasses and short hair\xe2\x80\x94 was holed up in her apartment. She and her partner set up to work side by side, their laptops perched on the kitchen island; Peck scoured the internet for news as Black people in America began dying from covid- 19 , the disease caused by the coronavirus, at twice the rate of whites. \xe2\x80\x9cI wanted to write something that would be valuable to readers and informative and empowering, particularly to Black audiences,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. So she did what so many other independent journalists were doing\xe2\x80\x94 she started a Substack. Substack, established in 2017 by three tech- and- media guys\xe2\x80\x94 Chris Best, Hamish McKenzie, and Jairaj Sethi\xe2\x80\x94 is a newsletter platform that allows writers and other creative types to distribute their work at tiered subscription rates. Newsletters go back at least as far as the Middle Ages, but these days, with full- time jobs at stable media companies evaporating\xe2\x80\x94 between the 2008 recession and 2019, newsroom employment dropped by 23 percent\xe2\x80\x94 Substack offers an appealing alternative. And, for many, it\xe2\x80\x99s a viable source of income. In three years, Substack\xe2\x80\x99s newsletters\xe2\x80\x94 covering almost every conceivable topic, from Australian Aboriginal rights to bread recipes to local Tennessee politics\xe2\x80\x94 have drawn more than two hundred fifty thousand paid subscribers. The top newsletter authors can earn six figures, an unheard- of amount for freelance journalists. Emily Atkin, who runs Heated , on the climate crisis, told me that her gross annual income surpassed $200,000\xe2\x80\x94 and among paid- readership Substacks, she\xe2\x80\x99s ranked fifteenth. \xe2\x80\x9cI literally opened my first savings account,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. Peck had been mulling the idea of starting a newsletter for a while. She began thinking seriously about Substack when she saw Beauty IRL , a newsletter by Darian Harvin. Like Peck, Harvin is a freelancer\xe2\x80\x94 it was \xe2\x80\x9creally a matter of time\xe2\x80\x9d until she was laid off from one media job or another, she figured\xe2\x80\x94 and she was using Substack as a place for surplus ideas. \xe2\x80\x9cI take some of my pitches and just write them for my newsletter,\xe2\x80\x9d Harvin said. \xe2\x80\x9cPublications are only paying me three hundred dollars per piece, so I thought, What would happen if I took some of them and grew my audience? \xe2\x80\x9d Her efforts were getting noticed; eventually, Substack gave her a $3,000 stipend and a $25,000 advance (in the latter arrangement, Substack takes 50 percent of her subscription fees until the advance is paid off, but if she doesn\xe2\x80\x99t reach that number, Harvin won\xe2\x80\x99t owe Substack the rest). Peck settled on a name for her project: Coronavirus News for Black Folks . She\xe2\x80\x99d produce it a few times a week, with a reading list of recommended articles and original interviews with Black essential workers, accompanied by images she\xe2\x80\x99d commission from Black illustrators. At least at the start, she decided, her newsletter would be free\xe2\x80\x94 she wanted her writing to be accessible, especially as Black people were suffering disproportionately from the pandemic- induced economic downturn. Her first installments were about Black men afraid of being racially profiled for wearing masks and coronavirus conspiracies circulating online; the tone was direct and conversational. (\xe2\x80\x9cAs Belcalis Marlenis Alm\xc3\xa1nzar famously said about covid- 19 , \xe2\x80\x98Sh*t is getting real,\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Peck wrote in an early dispatch.) After a couple weeks, Adriana Lacy, another Black journalist, interviewed Peck for her own Substack\xe2\x80\x94 The Intersection , focused on journalism, technology, and innovation\xe2\x80\x94 and Nieman Lab picked it up. A month into developing her newsletter, Peck had nine hundred seventy- eight subscribers. Not long after, that number rose to two thousand. Peck quickly recognized the possibilities of Substack: a wandering journalist, disenchanted by an industry that was never all that equitable to begin with and is now in financial free fall, could, perhaps, claim control of her work. As more people signed up for Coronavirus News for Black Folks , Peck imagined all the ways it might grow, and wondered whether it could become a full- time job. \xe2\x80\x9cIn an ideal world, I\xe2\x80\x99m the editor in chief or editor at large of the newsletter; I\xe2\x80\x99m using it to allow other journalists who like to cover these communities to have a place to write,\xe2\x80\x9d she mused. \xe2\x80\x9cAnd I\xe2\x80\x99m able to compensate them during a time when there are so many layoffs.\xe2\x80\x9d Patrice Peck When she started Coronavirus News for Black Folks, Peck was the sole producer of an ambitious project. S ubstack started the same way many media ventures do\xe2\x80\x94 with a personal essay. In 2017, Best, a programmer from a Vancouver suburb who cofounded a messaging app called Kik, had taken some time off work and, finding himself an avid reader, began thinking about writing something himself. He drafted a piece bemoaning how the journalism industry\xe2\x80\x99s failing business models incentivized clicks, retweets, and likes over incisive prose. At the time, the media apocalypse was in full force\xe2\x80\x94 the limits of digital media were apparent (that year, Mic and Vice instituted mass layoffs after bowing to Facebook in an ill- fated \xe2\x80\x9cpivot to video\xe2\x80\x9d) and legacy media was bleeding (Cond\xc3\xa9 Nast faced perennial ad revenue loss; a desperate Tribune Publishing changed its name to Tronc). \xe2\x80\x9cNow we\xe2\x80\x99re in this world where social media feeds optimize for engagement, because that\xe2\x80\x99s how they make money, and just as kind of an unintentional collateral damage they end up amplifying all the things that drive us crazy,\xe2\x80\x9d Best argued. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s bad for us as readers and bad for society.\xe2\x80\x9d Best sent a first draft to McKenzie, with whom he\xe2\x80\x99d worked at Kik. \xe2\x80\x9cHe was like, \xe2\x80\x98First of all, you\xe2\x80\x99re a bad writer and you shouldn\xe2\x80\x99t do this,\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Best recalled. In McKenzie\xe2\x80\x99s telling, he gently informed Best that he thought he was stating the obvious: everyone in media already understood what the problems were; what was missing was a solution. Before working in technology, McKenzie had grown up in a small town on the South Island of New Zealand and attended journalism school with the hope of becoming a foreign correspondent. He spent four years in Hong Kong, where he ended up writing mostly about indie music and drinking dens. Later, he worked as a reporter for PandoDaily , a tech news site, then transitioned into writing for companies\xe2\x80\x94 first Tesla, then Kik. McKenzie encouraged Best to think about more than a diagnosis. Through the spring of 2017, the two sent emails back and forth, had video calls, and brainstormed in Google Docs about what models might better serve journalism. Subscriptions, they decided, seemed the most promising\xe2\x80\x94 but not in the form of journals or magazines. \xe2\x80\x9cPaid newsletters\xe2\x80\x9d felt more familiar, personal, trustworthy\xe2\x80\x94 and more monetizable. They had good reason to think it could work. Best and McKenzie were both fans of Stratechery , the newsletter by Ben Thompson, a former employee of Apple and Microsoft based in Taipei, who since 2014 had been writing about tech full- time, charging readers directly. \xe2\x80\x9cThis guy was writing this newsletter from his bedroom in Taiwan and, as far as we know, making like a million dollars a year,\xe2\x80\x9d Best said. (An exaggeration, perhaps, but Thompson was earning a solid living.) They wondered why his approach, which took advantage of the internet\xe2\x80\x99s strengths\xe2\x80\x94 a global distribution network, easy payment systems\xe2\x80\x94 hadn\xe2\x80\x99t been replicated more widely. The Skimm and Axios had built companies around monetizing newsletters, but it wasn\xe2\x80\x99t an idea widely embraced by individual journalists. The guys devised a system of taking a 10 percent cut from subscriptions (Stripe, the credit card service that processed the fees, would take 2.9 percent, plus thirty cents per transaction), which they felt tied them to the writers. They reached out to Sethi, a developer they knew from Kik, to build out the technology. Their tagline was \xe2\x80\x9cWe literally only make money when the writers do.\xe2\x80\x9d Substack\xe2\x80\x99s mission, announced upon the company\xe2\x80\x99s official debut, sounded more grandiose: \xe2\x80\x9cWhen it has reached maturity, the subscription- based news industry could well be much larger than the newspaper business ever was, much like the ride- hailing industry in San Francisco is bigger than the taxi industry was before Lyft and Uber,\xe2\x80\x9d the founders wrote. \xe2\x80\x9cDemocratizing this subscription- based future will enable more writers to earn more money by writing about what truly matters.
